# Iridescence Is Crazy

**Source:** [Color Nerd](https://www.youtube.com/@ColorNerd1) (YouTube Shorts)
**Date:** 2025-01-27
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/shorts/hCGv73cA0yA
**Views:** 5,400

## Description

Physical demonstration of thin-film interference using iridescent acrylic sheets, showing how angle changes which wavelengths are blocked in reflection vs. transmission.

## Transcript

I have here a transparent piece of plastic — you can see my hand through it, right? And you can see light that falls on the wall bounces off and passes through the plastic back to your eye. So if light can pass through it, then how come it casts a shadow? And how come when I turn it this way it's opaque but it doesn't cast a shadow?

Here's the trick — I was using a red light. So now let's switch to a white one. The white light contains all the wavelengths of the visible spectrum, and as I shine it directly on this plastic, you can see that it's reflecting cyan. Cyan is the complement to red, so those long wavelengths that give us a red impression are actually getting blocked — which is why the shadow is also cyan.

But if I change the angle of the light — now because this sheet of plastic is iridescent, from this angle, even though the plastic is only failing to reflect long wavelengths, it's only failing to transmit medium wavelengths, which we see as green. And the complement of that is magenta.

## Key Concepts

- **Thin-film interference** — when a film is the right thickness, it cancels specific wavelengths through destructive interference
- **Angle dependence** — both the light angle and viewing angle affect which wavelengths are canceled
- **Reflection vs. transmission differ:**
  - Direct angle: reflects cyan (blocks red/long λ) → shadow is cyan
  - Changed angle: reflects differently but _transmits_ magenta (blocks green/mid λ)
  - The blocked wavelength band shifts with angle
- **Red light trick:** with monochromatic red light, the plastic appears transparent at one angle (red passes through) but opaque at another (red gets reflected) — no shadow when opaque because the blocked wavelength is reflected, not absorbed
- **Complementary pairs demonstrated:** red↔cyan, green↔magenta
